compiler_connection.notes: William Hooker is the compiler's direct paternal ancestor through the Hooker surname line. His father Seth (H00265) died when William was 2 years old. William is the link between the confirmed paternal line and the compiler's grandfather Michael Gay Hooker. Research needed to trace descendants.
compiler_connection.path_to_compiler: H00533 William → [descendants to trace] → Michael Gay Hooker → Samuel Talcott Hooker (compiler)

open_questions.notes: William born 1756 Kensington CT; died 1826. His starred children H01427-H01433 are the next research step. The compiler's grandfather Michael Gay Hooker's connection runs through one of these children.

military_service.unit: Capt. Caleb Bull's Company in Webb's Regiment, Continental Army
military_service.notes: Enlisted Apr 21, 1777 as Private for 3 years in Capt. Caleb Bull's Company, Webb's Regiment. Promoted Sergeant May 16, 1780 for an additional 8 months. Member of the Hartford 'band of music' (eight musicians) raised 1777 and attached to the Connecticut line. Pensioned Nov 4, 1818. DAR Ancestor #A057709.

narrative_blocks.header: Hartford band of music attached to Connecticut line
narrative_blocks.body: William Hooker was a member of the eight-man 'band of music' raised in Hartford in 1777 and attached to the Connecticut line of the Continental Army — one of the rare documented Revolutionary War regimental musical ensembles. The band provided drums, fifes, and field music during the war's middle years. The arrangement was unusual: most Revolutionary regiments had ad hoc field musicians rather than a formally organized eight-piece band.

narrative_blocks.header: Three years Private, eight months Sergeant 1777-1780
narrative_blocks.body: William enlisted April 21, 1777 as a Private in Captain Caleb Bull's Company of Webb's Regiment for a three-year term. On May 16, 1780 he was promoted to Sergeant for an additional eight months of service. He was pensioned November 4, 1818 (DAR Ancestor #A057709) — the federal pension secured nearly four decades after his service ended.

narrative_blocks.header: Nine children with Sarah Wilcox of Berlin
narrative_blocks.body: William married Sarah Wilcox of Berlin Connecticut and they had nine documented children together. The family remained in Kensington, the original Berlin Connecticut parish where William had been born in 1756 and where he would die in 1826. The nine-child household was characteristic of the post-Revolutionary Connecticut farming generation.

narrative_blocks.header: Kensington Berlin CT 1756 to 1826 age sixty-nine
narrative_blocks.body: William Hooker was born September 15, 1756 in Kensington (now Berlin) Connecticut and died February 4, 1826 in Kensington at age sixty-nine. He is buried at West Lane Cemetery in Berlin Connecticut. His Revolutionary War service spanned the war's middle period from the bleakest 1777 Valley Forge winter through the war's final 1780-1781 phase.

sources.full: Israel P. Warren D.D., The Stanley Families of America as Descended from John, Timothy, and Thomas Stanley of Hartford, CT, 1636 (1887)

sources.short: Find a Grave — South Dover Rural Cemetery

documents.document_blurb: 1838 letter from former Dover neighbor Mott Titus describing his Ohio migration, the canal boom, frontier economics, and the Whig sweep of New York's 1838 elections.

career.title: Physician
career.notes: Practiced medicine in South Dover Dutchess County NY for decades.

career.title: State Legislator
career.notes: Represented the town of Dover in the NY State Legislature; described as a prominent citizen.

narrative_blocks.header: South Dover physician served NY legislature
narrative_blocks.body: William left Berlin Connecticut for Dutchess County New York where he settled as a physician at South Dover. He became a prominent citizen and represented the town of Dover in the New York State Legislature. He died at Dover Furnace in March 1853 at age sixty-five.

narrative_blocks.header: He died at New Haven; she at Newark
narrative_blocks.body: Born in Berlin, Connecticut March 17, 1799, fourth child of William Hooker and Hannah Jones. He attended Yale and practiced medicine, marrying Eliza Beers — herself born 1799 — at New Haven on April 3, 1823. She died at Newark, New Jersey in 1862; he followed at New Haven on March 19, 1863, aged sixty-four, and was buried at Evergreen Cemetery.

narrative_blocks.header: Born and married two years apart
narrative_blocks.body: He and Eliza Beers were born the same year, 1799, married at New Haven in 1823, and died within a year of each other — she in 1862, he in 1863. The Berlin-born physician raised at least nine children before his death at New Haven.

burial.plot_notes: Evergreen Cemetery, New Haven, New Haven County CT.

narrative_blocks.header: Levi died young
narrative_blocks.body: Levi died in 1808 at the age of six.

cross_connections.display_label: His niece's son Abel Hollister came under his guardianship

narrative_blocks.header: Named for a brother who died nine days
narrative_blocks.body: His older brother John was born and died in December 1693 — living only nine days. When the next son arrived in 1695, his parents gave him the same name. He carried it through 71 years, Justice of the Peace and prominent Kensington businessman.

narrative_blocks.header: He and his three sisters all married into
narrative_blocks.body: His sisters Abigail, Mary, and Sarah married three sons of Capt. John Hart between 1719 and 1725. John himself had already married Mercy Hart in 1728 — from a different Hart branch, through Dea. Ebenezer Hart. Four Hooker siblings, two separate Hart lines, one generation.

narrative_blocks.header: Justice of the Peace
narrative_blocks.body: EH describes him as "a prominent and active man in the business of the town." He held the office of Justice of the Peace in Kensington, a parish of Berlin CT, and died there on August 3, 1766, age 71.

parents.father_research_notes: EH says father was Deacon Thomas Hart — WRONG. Barbour Collection Farmington VR confirms father was Dea. Ebenezer Hart (X00119). Primary source wins.

sources.notes: Barbour Collection confirms: father = Dea. Ebenezer Hart

bio.notes: EH gives 'Mercy (Mary) Hart' — both names used interchangeably

Research needed to confirm Mehitable Russell's Hooker descent line. Warren 1887 confirms: Elizabeth Standley married John Wadsworth — their son Daniel Wadsworth founded the Wadsworth Atheneum.

burial.plot_notes: Memento Mori Cemetery, Farmington, Hartford County, Connecticut. Same cemetery as his father Rev. Samuel Hooker (H00007).

notable.primary_url_label: Native Northeast Portal — John Hooker biography

narrative_blocks.header: Judge of Connecticut Colony's Supreme Court
narrative_blocks.body: He served as Judge of the Supreme Court of Connecticut Colony from 1724 to 1732 and as Speaker of the Lower House. He also served as witness, certifying Assistant, and Registrar in the 1733 Sue Squa land deed — three official roles in a single transaction.

narrative_blocks.header: He defended Tunxis land rights in court
narrative_blocks.body: In 1738, John Hooker provided a formal statement to the Connecticut General Court that ninety acres at Indian Neck had 'always belonged' to the Tunxis tribe — an unusual act of testimony on behalf of the indigenous people whose land his own ancestors had settled near.

narrative_blocks.header: The elm fell so someone could walk straight
narrative_blocks.body: His father gave him a site on the west side of Farmington's main street, where he built under the branches of a great elm. The tree became famous through all the region for the company that gathered beneath it — three generations of Hookers lived in its shade before a later owner cut it down to straighten the sidewalk.

narrative_blocks.header: The bell tolled — Hooker stepped out first
narrative_blocks.body: A chronicler recorded that every Sunday the bell rang, then began tolling; at the first stroke, Hooker issued from his house and proceeded to the Meeting House. The minister walked from his house. Whoever arrived first waited at the door; together they entered — at which the bell stopped and all the congregation arose.

narrative_blocks.header: The Sabbath ended when Hooker touched his gate
narrative_blocks.body: The holiness of Sunday was supposed to end at sunset — but no child in the town dared stir until John Hooker gave the signal. When the sun sank, he walked to his gate and leaned on it; open flew the doors and out came the children with a wild rush and shout.

narrative_blocks.header: Dark hair, dark eyes — dignity commanding respect
narrative_blocks.body: His grandson described him: "Dark hair and dark eyes, and a dignity in his air and expression that procured profound respect." A fine looking man, tall, very erect, and had a prominent large nose. He lived his entire life in Farmington, dying there in 1746 at age 81.

Their families united — three daughters married Hart brothers.
Her sister Elizabeth Standley married into the Wadsworth line.

narrative_blocks.header: Her father won distinction in the Indian wars
narrative_blocks.body: Capt. John Standley was a man of wealth and high social position who had served as lieutenant and captain in the Indian wars. Her sister Elizabeth Standley's granddaughter married Col. Jeremiah Wadsworth, Commissary General of the Continental Army — Wadsworth's wife also descended from Rev. Thomas Hooker, making Wadsworth's children doubly connected to the Hooker line.

bio.notes: Also spelled 'Stanley' in EH entry 30; 'Standley' in entries 99-101

open_questions.question: Deacon Thomas Hart vs Deacon Ebenezer Hart — identity of Mercy Hart's father
open_questions.notes: EH entry 98 gives Mercy Hart's father as 'Deacon Thomas Hart'. FVR (Barbour Collection) gives 'John, Jr., m. Mary HEART, d. Dea. Ebenezer' — suggesting Deacon Ebenezer Hart. Could be two different Deacons, or EH/FVR error on first name. Andrews' Stephen Hart and his Descendants should resolve this.

burialCemetery.notes: Kensington is a parish of Berlin, CT — sources may list burial location as either 'Kensington' or 'Berlin'. John Hooker No. 2 (H00098) and Mercy Hart Hooker (I00041) buried here. Burial site of Samuel Hooker (H00086) and family
